The European Legacy, Vol. 10, No. 2, pp. 149–162, 2005



          Alexander von Humboldt’s Invention of the
                      Natural Landscape

                                              Chunglin Kwa




Abstract Landscape took on a new meaning through the new science of plant geography of Alexander von

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
tural Landscape

                                              Chunglin Kwa




Abstract Landscape took on a new meaning through the new science of plant geography of Alexander von
Humboldt (1769–1857). In the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, ‘‘landscape’’ was foremost a painterly
genre. Slowly, painted landscapes came to bear on natural surroundings, but by 1800 it was still not common
to designate sites as ‘‘landscapes.’’ Humboldt looked at plant vegetation with a painterly gaze. Artists,
according to him, could suggest in their work that an abstract unity lay hidden underneath observable
phenomena. Humboldt projected painted landscapes on nature and found its ecological unity. By doing so, he
ultimately stripped the concept of landscape from its primary visual meaning.


      When Alexander von Humboldt (1769–1857) travelled through the jungles and
plains of Spanish America, what did he see? I will argue in this article that Humboldt saw
landscapes and that he was the first or among the very first to do so. But in his writings in
German, Humboldt did not use Landschaft to describe what he saw.1 ‘‘Landscape’’ as a

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
apes and that he was the first or among the very first to do so. But in his writings in
German, Humboldt did not use Landschaft to describe what he saw.1 ‘‘Landscape’’ as a
designation of a natural landscape, like a heath or a prairie, is less old than perhaps we are
inclined to think. In the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, ‘‘landscape’’ was foremost
a painterly genre. Only gradually was the imagery of landscape paintings used as a
metaphor for what could be observed in the outer world.
      Humboldt had been Inspector of Mines in the service of Prussia, but withdrew from
government service when, at the age of 27, he received his family inheritance. From 1799
to 1804 he travelled in the Americas. Upon his return to Europe, he enjoyed immense
fame as the climber of the Chimborazo, at the time held as the world’s highest mountain.
His travelogues and other publications, in which he expounded the new science of plant
geography, would keep him in the public eye until the end of his life.
      In this article, I will discuss how a painterly gaze shaped Humboldt’s scientific work.

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
ence of plant
geography, would keep him in the public eye until the end of his life.
      In this article, I will discuss how a painterly gaze shaped Humboldt’s scientific work.
Plant geography gave landscape a new meaning, but to appreciate its importance, we
must first look at the evolution of the concept of ‘‘landscape’’ in the seventeenth and
eighteenth centuries. Humboldt exploited the visual qualities of what he saw as good
landscape painting to the full, and in the process transformed the concept of landscape
from an aesthetic category into an abstract entity.




University of Amsterdam, Department of Political Sciences, Oudezijds Achterburgwal 237, Amsterdam, The Netherlands.
Email: c.l.kwa@uva.nl

ISSN 1084–8770 print/ISSN 1470–1316 online/05/020149–14 ß 2005 International Society for the Study of European Ideas
DOI: 10.1080/1084877052000330084
150          Chunglin Kwa

                   The Classical Landscape and the Garden
     How did a seventeenth or eighteenth-century person understand a landscape
painting? Not as the depiction of a natural landscape, as we understand it today.

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
cape and the Garden
     How did a seventeenth or eighteenth-century person understand a landscape
painting? Not as the depiction of a natural landscape, as we understand it today.
Insofar as the classical landscape depicted something, it was a garden. Real nature was
considered lacking in symmetry and other aesthetic qualities. In turn, gardens referred
to landscape paintings. A classical education was required to enjoy an English
landscape garden at the beginning of the eighteenth century, or else one would not
be able to see the allusions to Poussin and Claude, Horace and Virgil, and to Milton’s
Paradise Lost.2
     The word ‘‘landscape’’ entered the English language as a loan word from the Dutch,
along with a fair amount of landscape paintings imported from Holland and Flanders.
The English critic Henry Peacham wrote in 1606:
      Landtskip is a Dutch word and it is as much as wee shoulde say in english landship, or
      expressing of the land by hills, woodes, Castles, seas, valleys, ruines, hanging rocks,
      Citties, Townes, andc. as farre as may bee shewed within our Horizon.3

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
ship, or
      expressing of the land by hills, woodes, Castles, seas, valleys, ruines, hanging rocks,
      Citties, Townes, andc. as farre as may bee shewed within our Horizon.3

In Dutch, the word had originally referred to rural settlements and had administrative
connotations. But by the end of the sixteenth century, ‘‘landschap’’ had acquired a new
meaning: a pictorial representation, first as the background on a history piece, later as an
independent genre. Landschap is henceforth an ‘‘expressing,’’ a representation of the land,
especially as inhabited by people.4 Peacham encourages artists to travel and to make
drawings after life. But their landscapes should be the result of ‘‘invention,’’ in which
topographical exactness was not the goal.
       Paradise Lost contains descriptions of ‘‘landskips,’’ but they do not refer to what can
be observed in the open air. Milton does with poetry what a painter does with paint. His
landscapes are verbal paintings, with a rather well circumscribed iconography.5 There is
some affinity here with the concept of the picturesque. In 1604, Carel van Mander used

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
His
landscapes are verbal paintings, with a rather well circumscribed iconography.5 There is
some affinity here with the concept of the picturesque. In 1604, Carel van Mander used
this word (schilderachtig) to describe in an appreciative way the use of expressive language
by a poet rendering a painted landscape.6
       Landscape elements can be picturesque, too; in other words, they can be worthy
enough to be painted. The Dutch painter Gerard de Lairesse, in 1707, gave as an example
‘‘whole trees, standing straight, round of stem and of modest leaves and crown.’’7 Nature
itself did not always meet such demands. Barren rocks and unkempt hedges convinced
many onlookers that art was superior to nature.8
       Seventeenth-century Dutch landscapes, most importantly those by Jacob van
Ruisdael and Jan van Goyen, enjoy a reputation of being ‘‘realistic.’’ Several Dutch art
historians have attacked the notion of realism. Josua Bruyn and E. de Jongh have argued
that landscapes and genre pieces, while at first appearing as realistic, are in fact allegories.9
Bruyn has construed several landscapes by Ruisdael as images of ‘‘a transient world, where

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
pes and genre pieces, while at first appearing as realistic, are in fact allegories.9
Bruyn has construed several landscapes by Ruisdael as images of ‘‘a transient world, where
the lonely traveler, beset by sinful temptations, may hope for eternal bliss after death.’’10
Bruyn’s procedure is to reveal the allegorical meaning of several individual elements in
the painted landscape and to construct a narrative around them. While there indeed seems
no basis to argue for realism on the level of the landscape as a whole, Bruyn’s argument is
not directed against the realistic aspect of the individual elements making up the
landscape.
                                        Humboldt’s Invention of the Natural Landscape        151

      Iconographer De Jongh bases his argument against realism on certain paintings
by Ruisdael and by Hercules Seghers. On one landscape by the latter a few typical
Amsterdam canal houses can be seen, loosely set in a rocky Scandinavian landscape.
Ruisdael painted Bentheim castle (near Rheine, in Germany) on a hill much higher than
it was in actual fact.
      Convincing as their argument may be, the question whether this constitutes proof

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
d Bentheim castle (near Rheine, in Germany) on a hill much higher than
it was in actual fact.
      Convincing as their argument may be, the question whether this constitutes proof
against the realistic motives of the Dutch painters is beset with difficulties. I would argue
that Ruisdael and Seghers did paint realistically, but that their realism is of a very different
kind than what is generally understood by the term as it was used in the nineteenth
century. A parallel with literature can serve to illustrate this point. As Erich Auerbach has
shown, both Henry IV by Shakespeare and Le Père Goriot by Balzac are realistic in a certain
way. But we cannot apply Balzac’s sociological criteria of realism, as found in the first half
of the nineteenth century, to Henry IV. Shakespeare is realistic on many details of
ordinary life, but for their coherence we must look at a level of spiritual transcendence
rather than at societal structure.11
      Seghers and Ruisdael are both realistic with respect to the various entities they
render in paint. For a spectator in the time of the Baroque, it was satisfactory if both

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
.11
      Seghers and Ruisdael are both realistic with respect to the various entities they
render in paint. For a spectator in the time of the Baroque, it was satisfactory if both
hill and castle were realistic.12 But a modern observer wants the whole of it, their
combination, to be correct. It would therefore be grossly anachronistic to expect
ecological realism from Ruisdael. The nineteenth century made this mistake, because its
avant-garde began to work ‘‘without a subject.’’13 No longer being able to read
allegories, it could claim Ruisdael as a precursor.


                               Landscape as Metaphor
     One could imagine that a seventeenth-century or early-eighteenth-century person
could experience natural scenery ‘‘just like on a painting,’’ and so, with or without the
use of the word itself, designate it as a landscape. Writing in 1712, Joseph Addison,
theorist of the picturesque, wrote:
     If the natural Embroidery of the Meadows were helpt and improved by some additions
     of Art, and the several Rows of Hedges set off by Trees and Flowers . . . a Man might
     make a pretty Landskip of his own possessions.14

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
lpt and improved by some additions
     of Art, and the several Rows of Hedges set off by Trees and Flowers . . . a Man might
     make a pretty Landskip of his own possessions.14

This kind of metaphorical usage became more common later in the eighteenth century,15
but Addison’s idea was first realized in eighteenth-century British parks and gardens. The
resemblance of several views in the garden at Stourhead to paintings by Claude and by
Poussin is striking, and one of the most famous British park designers, William Kent,
started his career as a landscape painter.16
      Salvator Rosa (1615–73) inspired many to ‘‘see’’ landscapes in nature. Working in
Rome at the same time as Poussin and Claude, he resisted their influence and example,
and developed a style of landscape painting that clashed with classicist ideals. Lairesse may
have referred to Ruisdael or Hobbema, yet his words of disapproval apply even more
aptly to Rosa: ‘‘ragged-looking trees, from which branches and leaves thrust ferociously
east and west, without design; their trunks twisted, old, and split, sprouting many knots
and cavities.’’17
152          Chunglin Kwa

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
ch branches and leaves thrust ferociously
east and west, without design; their trunks twisted, old, and split, sprouting many knots
and cavities.’’17
152          Chunglin Kwa

      Rosa’s biography would have appealed to the Romantics: he preferred to live in
poverty and to stay independent, rather than enjoy the riches and protection of a
patron.18 Fifty years after his death, he was discovered by the British, when Sir Joshua
Reynolds gave him an, albeit secondary, place in the neoclassical canon. In 1740, Horace
Walpole, travelling through the Alps on his way to Italy, saw: ‘‘precipices, mountains,
torrents, wolves, rumblings, Salvator Rosa!’’19 The German naturalist Georg Forster, on
board the Resolution, saw on New Zealand’s coasts ‘‘rude sceneries in the style of Rosa.’’20
Walpole and other British collectors appreciated in Rosa a rendering of the sublime, as
distinct from the aesthetical category of the beautiful. They looked at his work with a
pre-Romantic gaze.21
      On scientific expeditions like those of Cook in the Pacific, nature emancipated itself
from art and became a model for art itself. Exotic nature especially demanded new criteria

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
On scientific expeditions like those of Cook in the Pacific, nature emancipated itself
from art and became a model for art itself. Exotic nature especially demanded new criteria
of judgment. Newly found grottoes with their huge stalagmites and stalactites made
people forget the artistic grottoes of the Renaissance.22 The new taste developed
simultaneously with a growing preference for the sublime in nature. In Europe, the Alps
and later the Pyrenees, would benefit from this change in the last quarter of the
eighteenth century,23 which was also when Humboldt began his journey through
Spanish America. By then Gilbert White, author of Natural History of Selborne (1789), had
seen ‘‘Italian skies’’ in his rural Hampshire and ‘‘scenes worthy of the pencil of a
Rubens.’’24 It was observations of this kind that gave rise to the concept of the natural
landscape.
      But White was not to accomplish this himself; indeed, there are only few such
observations in his Natural History. In a sense, White missed an opportunity. He had
requested the Swiss-born artist Samuel Hieronymus Grimm to draw several views of

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
only few such
observations in his Natural History. In a sense, White missed an opportunity. He had
requested the Swiss-born artist Samuel Hieronymus Grimm to draw several views of
Selborne to be included in his book. Grimm, known for his faithful topographical
drawings, produced a dozen sketches. In private letters, White showed himself satisfied
and included several of them as illustrations, but, tellingly, he nowhere reflects on their
images in the text.25


                                Humboldt’s Holistic Gaze
      In his essay ‘‘Ideen zu einer Physiognomik der Gewächse,’’ which he wrote shortly
after returning from the Americas, Humboldt teaches his readers how to see:
      He who knows how to comprehend nature at a single glance and knows how to
      abstract from local phenomena, will easily perceive . . .
He would later repeat this in his Cosmos:
      The scattered images offered to the contemplation of the senses, notwithstanding their
      number and diversity, were gradually fused into a concrete whole. . . . Terrestrial
      nature was conceived in its generality.26
This is almost like an echo of Rousseau. Rousseau discovered during his lonely

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
dually fused into a concrete whole. . . . Terrestrial
      nature was conceived in its generality.26
This is almost like an echo of Rousseau. Rousseau discovered during his lonely
wanderings in old age that a sensitive observer like himself ceases to discern, at a certain
point in time, the individual flowers and plants that at first he had set out to study:
      All individual objects escape him; he sees and feels nothing but the unity of all things.27
                                           Humboldt’s Invention of the Natural Landscape               153

Rousseau, in 1782, expressed a completely new way of experiencing nature. But he did
not yet have the vocabulary to articulate the unity that he perceived. Humboldt did. The
first quotation continues:
     He who comprehends nature at a single glance, and knows how to abstract from single
     phenomena, will easily perceive how organic force and the abundance of vital
     developments increase with the increase of heat from the poles to the equator.
     . . . Every zone of vegetation has, besides its own attractions, a peculiar character. . . . As

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
developments increase with the increase of heat from the poles to the equator.
     . . . Every zone of vegetation has, besides its own attractions, a peculiar character. . . . As
     in different organic beings we recognize a distinct physiognomy . . . so there is also a
     certain physiognomy of nature exclusively peculiar to each portion of the earth.28
Humboldt’s technical words are physiognomy and vegetational zone. Every zone has its own
physiognomy, a concept which, as we will see, he may have borrowed from Lavater, and
which designated the science of studying human faces. Humboldt himself distinguished
16 zones on earth, but his followers would later multiply this figure several times. He does
not use ‘‘landscape’’ for natural scenery in the following crucial passage:
     The idea which the artist wishes to indicate by the expressions, ‘‘Swiss nature,’’ or
     ‘‘Italian skies,’’ is based on a dark sense of some local characteristic.
Humboldt’s gaze is the gaze of a painter: ‘‘to apprehend these characteristics, and to
reproduce them visibly, is the province of landscape painting.’’29 At the end of his essay,

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
’s gaze is the gaze of a painter: ‘‘to apprehend these characteristics, and to
reproduce them visibly, is the province of landscape painting.’’29 At the end of his essay,
Humboldt observes that a work describing in detail the 16 vegetational zones would be
very useful to an artist. We have come full circle: at first Humboldt learned from the
artists; now he can be their teacher. Humboldt’s influence on art has been fairly
important, as we shall see, but even more interesting is what Humboldt took from the art
of painting. We may note at this point that it was not by accident that Humboldt did
not use ‘‘landscape’’ to designate a vegetational zone. ‘‘Landscape’’ was still an exclusively
painterly concept, a genre. Recently, Simon Schama observed that ‘‘landscapes are
culture before they are nature; constructions of the imagination projected onto wood and
water and rock.’’30 Humboldt projected painted landscapes on nature, and what he found
was its ecological unity.
       Eventually, the concept of landscape could be used without giving a thought to
a representation on canvas. K. Rosenkrantz, a German geographer, defines the word in

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
al unity.
       Eventually, the concept of landscape could be used without giving a thought to
a representation on canvas. K. Rosenkrantz, a German geographer, defines the word in
1850 as follows:
     Landscapes are relative wholes, stepwise integrated local systems of factors comprising
     all realms of nature.31
No mention of art here.


                        Landscape as an EcologicalWhole
     Humboldt’s views and practice are essentially different from those of his eighteenth-
century precursors. Karl Ludwig Willdenow, and, of course, Linnaeus, had been
botanists, or florists above all. They collected and classified individual plants. Floras were
delineated geographically in a fairly arbitrary way until the end of the eighteenth century.
Although the florists were certainly interested in a plant’s habitat, its place of residence, its
indication served them primarily to find a particular plant quickly.
154         Chunglin Kwa

      Humboldt’s radically different view can be illustrated by the idea of the ‘‘heath,’’
which he understood very differently from Willdenow. Willdenow took the heath as

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
Humboldt’s radically different view can be illustrated by the idea of the ‘‘heath,’’
which he understood very differently from Willdenow. Willdenow took the heath as
a topographical indication, namely, the place where Calluna vulgaris (heather) can be
found. Humboldt saw the heath as an association of species, of Erica (Calluna) vulgaris,
Erica tetralix (cross-leaved heather), and two lichens. The crucial word here is association,
the social bond between four species, unrelated taxonomically.32 In this way, Humboldt
transformed the heath into a landscape, in the modern, ecological sense.33
      In contemporary history of science, it has become customary to link similar
revolutions in thought with Romanticism, or, alternatively and in Foucauldian terms,
with the transition from the classical to the modern episteme. Among others, we may
mention briefly Cuvier’s discovery of the unity of plan of the anatomy of vertebrates and
Oersted’s discovery of the link between electricity and magnetism, originating in his
conviction of the unity of natural forces. Humboldt was not simply the child of his time,

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
nd
Oersted’s discovery of the link between electricity and magnetism, originating in his
conviction of the unity of natural forces. Humboldt was not simply the child of his time,
but engaged actively in the development of philosophical Romanticism in Germany.
His friend Schiller had articulated the notion of ‘‘aesthetic experience’’ as a necessary
condition for a correct concept of freedom, citizenship, the state, or in one word, of
anything for which a vision of ‘‘the whole’’ was a prerequisite. Although his friendship
with Schiller was not always easy, Humboldt remained true to these ideas throughout
his life.34


                 What Humboldt Learned from the Artists
      From which artists had Humboldt learned to see and how had his taste been formed?
Humboldt’s travelogues are infused with admiration for sublime nature. Without ever
using the word, Humboldt reports with apparent enthusiasm about regions on earth
which in the eighteenth century were typically seen as sublime (and not as beautiful), and
the words that he did use were commonly associated with the sublime. The spatial

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
on earth
which in the eighteenth century were typically seen as sublime (and not as beautiful), and
the words that he did use were commonly associated with the sublime. The spatial
expanse of the pampas is ‘‘immeasurable,’’ calling forth a ‘‘feeling of infiniteness,’’ and
evoking ‘‘emotions of a higher spiritual order.’’35
      But Humboldt could not make do without art in his appreciation of natural scenery.
Travelling along the coast of Venezuela, a miraculously long sea-arm reminded him of
the paysage fantastique on the background of Leonardo da Vinci’s Mona Lisa.36
      His is a most explicit aesthetic gaze, and at several places in his work he discusses
landscape painting. He owned a considerable number of landscape paintings and knew a
number of artists personally. Ingres had given him a work as a gift. Apparently, Humboldt
ignored several well known painters, among them Constable, Turner, Friedrich, and
Corot. Largely on this basis, Humboldt’s taste has recently been deemed as conservative,
neo-classicist rather than romantic.37 Humboldt did not favour open air painting, but he

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
Corot. Largely on this basis, Humboldt’s taste has recently been deemed as conservative,
neo-classicist rather than romantic.37 Humboldt did not favour open air painting, but he
did support the making of coloured sketches directly from nature, so that the artist would
have realistic details at his disposal.38 Apparently, and in conformity with the prevalent
taste at the time, he thought it proper that a painting be made in the studio.
      Humboldt admired the Dutch landscape painters of the seventeenth century, most
importantly Ruisdael and Allaert van Everdingen. In the grouping of trees by such artists,
Humboldt recognized a feeling for the whole from particulars, such as the typical image
of intertwined foliage. He also liked the more documentary style of Frans Post. Post was
                                      Humboldt’s Invention of the Natural Landscape      155

an architect in the retinue of governor Johan Maurits van Nassau, during the latter’s
Brazilian colonial adventure on behalf of the Dutch, from 1636 to 1644. Humboldt
praised Post for his delicate depiction of the form of the coast, the ground and the

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
the latter’s
Brazilian colonial adventure on behalf of the Dutch, from 1636 to 1644. Humboldt
praised Post for his delicate depiction of the form of the coast, the ground and the
vegetation, and credited him as the first artist to work from actual observation.39
       If Humboldt valued the Dutch for their realism, he respected the classicists for their
unity of composition. William Hodges mattered most to him. Hodges had been in the
service of captain James Cook as landscape painter, during the latter’s second voyage, on
the Resolution, from 1772 to 1774. In 1790, at the age of 21, Humboldt had paid a short
visit to London, and no doubt had seen Hodges’ paintings on that occasion. In Cosmos he
would later describe how these had evoked in him the desire to see the tropics.40 He had
been in London in the company of his friend and colleague Georg Forster, who had also
been with Cook on the second voyage in the capacity of botanist and naturalist.
       Hodges had specialized in landscape painting and had received his education in the
style of classical idealism. On board the Resolution, he was assigned the task of drawing

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
Hodges had specialized in landscape painting and had received his education in the
style of classical idealism. On board the Resolution, he was assigned the task of drawing
coastal profiles. In a sense, coastal views are landscapes, but topographical accurateness is
crucial in their case, because they were made to assist captains in identifying unknown
lands in future sea voyages. No wonder Hodges’ style evolved to attain a greater
naturalism.41 Several of Hodges’ paintings made during the voyage testify to an apparent
interest in meteorological conditions, and his Tahitian landscapes are impregnated with a
peculiar light, the result of his efforts to render the special atmospheric conditions in the
tropics. We may note that the Royal Society, Cook’s patron, wanted to be informed
about extreme weather conditions, which is why the Resolution had a meteorologist on
board.
       Hodges had wanted to elevate the exotic landscape to the status of the classical
tradition of Poussin, Claude, and Rosa, but in this, he did not succeed. In 1795, he fell
out of favour with the Prince of Wales, on the suspicion of supporting the French

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
lassical
tradition of Poussin, Claude, and Rosa, but in this, he did not succeed. In 1795, he fell
out of favour with the Prince of Wales, on the suspicion of supporting the French
Revolution, and thereafter no-one dared to buy his work. More recently, art historians
have passed severe judgments on his work. In his monograph on neo-classicism, Hugh
Honour pokes fun at Hodges’ Monuments of Easter Island, for its alleged similarity to
classical Greek ruins.42
       Humboldt, however, was impressed by Hodges. No doubt he perceived in his work
a quality that he would later hold as an example to other artists: a combination of, on the
one hand, unity in composition and atmosphere, and attention to individual plants and
minerals, on the other.


                             The Abstract Individual
      It is a paradox that what Humboldt found really important in natural scenery could
only be discovered by scientists. Artists could feel and suggest in their work that an
abstract unity lay hidden underneath observable phenomena, hence the dark feeling.
Insofar as this unity could be rendered visible, the unity was not visibly out there, just as

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
an
abstract unity lay hidden underneath observable phenomena, hence the dark feeling.
Insofar as this unity could be rendered visible, the unity was not visibly out there, just as
no anatomical illustration can depict what an anatomist sees when he dissects a body.
Humboldt’s Tableau physique des Andes et des pays voisins (Physical Portrait of the Andes
and adjacent lands), organized around the Chimborazo, is an attempt to give a visual
representation of the invisible. Among the wealth of measurement data brought together
156          Chunglin Kwa

in the Profile, we find those made with a cyanometer, with which Humboldt could
ascertain the degree of blueness of the sky. A term such as ‘‘Italian sky’’ was commonly
used in art, but Humboldt wanted to give it a physical footing.
      The Andes Portrait earned Humboldt admiration, but also ridicule. It seemed overly
superficial to merely put all the measurements side by side, as this could not lead to real
unity. But Humboldt had pre-empted this kind of critique. According to him, the
Physical Portrait was a first approximation. Eventually it would be possible to draw a

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
lead to real
unity. But Humboldt had pre-empted this kind of critique. According to him, the
Physical Portrait was a first approximation. Eventually it would be possible to draw a
Portrait (Naturgemälde) of a higher, naturphilosophisches, kind.43 And we should grant
Humboldt that, much later, ecology too would not be very successful in generating
a style of illustrations other than the mostly dull food webs and system diagrams that can
be found in ecological textbooks.
      Humboldt’s use of the word naturphilosophisch shows once again his commitment
to philosophical programmes such as those of Schiller and Schelling, although as an
empiricist he was at times barely tolerated by his idealist friends. Schelling was infamously
known for his view that not many scientific facts were needed to construct the system of
nature. Writing at a time when the backlash against the Naturphilosophie had made itself
felt in Germany, Humboldt upholds it at old age.44
      It has been argued that Humboldt solved the problem of theory and data, the
relationship between his philosophical-aesthetic view of nature and his commitment to

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
t old age.44
      It has been argued that Humboldt solved the problem of theory and data, the
relationship between his philosophical-aesthetic view of nature and his commitment to
measurement, by relegating each to different chapters of human knowledge. That would
explain the literary form of the Cosmos and the Views of Nature, in which the aesthetic
discourse of the main text addresses itself to the lay reader, and extended footnotes cater
to the needs of the scientifically educated.45 I find it more plausible, though, that there is a
similar solution to Cosmos as to the Chimborazo Portrait: real unity would come later.46
      In his introduction to Cosmos, Humboldt writes:
      This science of the Cosmos is not, however, to be regarded as a mere encyclopaedic
      aggregation of the most important and general results that have been collected together
      from special branches of knowledge. These results are nothing more than the materials
      for a vast edifice . . . In the work before us, partial facts will be considered only in
      relation to the whole.47
Humboldt approaches the whole via the aesthetic gaze and a ‘‘picturesque’’ style of

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
work before us, partial facts will be considered only in
      relation to the whole.47
Humboldt approaches the whole via the aesthetic gaze and a ‘‘picturesque’’ style of
writing.48 His use of the concept of physiognomy is a prime example thereof. He
circumscribes the idea of physiognomy by a simile: the knowledge of minerals stands to
the science of mountain ranges as the individual description of nature to the general, that
is, the physiognomy of nature.49 (It is noteworthy that Humboldt mentions as most
outstanding in the description of particular scenery—einzelne Himmelsstriche—writers, not
painters: Forster, Goethe, Bernardin de St Pierre, Chateaubriand).
      But how to arrive at whatever is general in the regions of the world? First, says
Humboldt, we have to know what contributes most to the formation of an impression of
the whole. There can be no doubt, according to him, that this is the vegetation, not the
air, or the composition of the soil. Humboldt then continues:
      If in one glance, one encompasses all 80,000 plant species, then we find that this
      prodigious quantity presents some few forms to which most of the others may be

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
inues:
      If in one glance, one encompasses all 80,000 plant species, then we find that this
      prodigious quantity presents some few forms to which most of the others may be
      referred. In determining those forms, on whose individual beauty, distribution, and
      grouping, the physiognomy of a country’s vegetation depends, we must not ground
                                         Humboldt’s Invention of the Natural Landscape            157

     our opinion . . . on the smaller organs of propagation, that is, the blossoms and fruit;
     but must be guided solely by those elements of magnitude and mass from which the
     total impression of a district receives its character of individuality.50

Humboldt then returns to the artists:
     Where vegetable forms occur in large masses, the outlines and distribution of the
     leaves, and the forms of the stems and the branches lose their individuality and blend
     together. The painter—and here his delicate artistical appreciation of nature comes
     especially into play—distinguishes between pines or palms and beeches in the

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
blend
     together. The painter—and here his delicate artistical appreciation of nature comes
     especially into play—distinguishes between pines or palms and beeches in the
     background of a landscape, but not between forests of beech and other thickly foliated
     trees.51

Artists have the ability to abstract beech trees or oaks in a landscape with deciduous trees
(but it would be useful, says Humboldt in a different passage, if they would give an
indication of botanical details in the foreground of a painting). Deciduous trees may
be the distinguishing aspect of a certain vegetational zone (or natural landscape, or
ecosystem), and hence determine its physiognomy, which through them acquires its own
individuality. Every single vegetational zone is an individual, a different kind of individual
than a single tree or animal, but still an individual. How does Humboldt ‘‘know’’ this?
      Humboldt did not proceed via the formation of hypotheses. At no point does
Humboldt build an argument by way of constructing a proposition about, for instance,
the tropical vegetational zone, deriving from it the existence of specific relationships

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
t does
Humboldt build an argument by way of constructing a proposition about, for instance,
the tropical vegetational zone, deriving from it the existence of specific relationships
between plants and/or animals, and proposing to verify them empirically. Nothing of the
kind.52 Humboldt proceeded via abstraction:
     By the suppression of all unnecessary detail, the great masses are better seen, and the
     reasoning faculty is enabled to grasp all that might otherwise escape the limited range of
     the senses.53

The vegetational zone (or the landscape) is an individual of a more general order, a higher
order individual. Abstraction, guided by intuition, leads unmediated to the higher, the
general.54 Humboldt was not alone in upholding this view. Goethe had observed before
him that ‘‘true poetry’’ is made by the one who sees the general in the particular.55 And it
seems likely that Humboldt knew the work of an important theoretician of the process of
abstraction: Johann Caspar Lavater (1741–1801).
      Lavater, according to Barbara Maria Stafford, is an important representative of neo-

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
mportant theoretician of the process of
abstraction: Johann Caspar Lavater (1741–1801).
      Lavater, according to Barbara Maria Stafford, is an important representative of neo-
Platonism, an influential philosophical view in the era of neo-classicism.56 Lavater was the
best-known practitioner of physiognomics, the science that studies the outer features of
the face, the face being the mirror of the soul. The visible was studied in order to see the
unseen. Lavater demanded a systematic connoisseurship. The point of departure was
always the individual, the particular. Lavater, who also wrote a treatise on landscape
painting, thought it impermissible not to be able to ascertain to which species depicted
trees belonged.57 And he made the same point regarding faces.
      One of the preferred techniques of physiognomics was the silhouette drawing. The
silhouette is individual and abstract at the same time, not just an idealization. Voltaire in
silhouette remains Voltaire, not ‘‘a wise man’’ in general. But Voltaire’s silhouette should
enable the onlooker to see his characteristic features, among them wisdom.
158         Chunglin Kwa

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
aire, not ‘‘a wise man’’ in general. But Voltaire’s silhouette should
enable the onlooker to see his characteristic features, among them wisdom.
158         Chunglin Kwa

      So what the silhouette is for the science of studying faces, the landscape painting is
for the physiognomy of a region. This is a paradox, because the relationships that
Humboldt discovered in the natural landscape cannot be seen. The fact that there is a
social relationship between two heather species and two lichens is not immediately
obvious upon inspection. But the social relationships as they exist engender specific ratios
of number and mass between the species, and this leads to the specific look of the
vegetation. It is this look that can be depicted in a landscape painting.
      In Cosmos, Humboldt criticizes some of his ‘‘distinguished’’ German botanist
friends who thought that the character of the flora of, for instance, Mexico or European
Russia, is determined by the larger number of one or two natural families of plant species.
‘‘In my opinion,’’ says Humboldt, it ‘‘consists in the more complicated relations of the

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
etermined by the larger number of one or two natural families of plant species.
‘‘In my opinion,’’ says Humboldt, it ‘‘consists in the more complicated relations of the
co-existence of many families, and in the relative numerical value of their species.’’58
Again, Humboldt sees a social relationship as the deeper cause of the geographical
distribution of plants.
      Thus, the aesthetic point of view provided Humboldt with an important argument
against the traditional methods of the florists and also supported his own holistic view.
The aesthetic gaze is the direct precursor of the abstract, the space of (romantic) science.



                 What the Artists Learned from Humboldt
      For Humboldt, it was not good enough to render a landscape as it was. A landscape
was an idea, and it was this that a landscape painting had to convey. Didactic
considerations were never far from Humboldt’s mind. Not only did he wish to incite
young travellers to make scientific journeys in the tropics but he also aimed at educating
the public. Spectators could stay home while enjoying painted panoramas and ‘‘the

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
ite
young travellers to make scientific journeys in the tropics but he also aimed at educating
the public. Spectators could stay home while enjoying painted panoramas and ‘‘the
discoveries of Prevost and Daguerre.’’59 He nowhere reflects on tourism, but it is unlikely
that he would have disapproved of it. Paintings and drawings of landscapes in the
nineteenth century would indeed boost tourism, like the drawings of William Green that
brought many visitors to the Lake District.60 Whereas the landscapes of Claude and
Poussin had been read in the eighteenth century as private landscapes, the new landscapes
were public.
      Humboldt’s programme in painting found followers fairly soon after the publication
of Ansichten, early in the nineteenth century. One example is the Dutch king, as patron
of the arts and sciences. William I knew Humboldt personally. In 1815, William I
sent Caspar Reinwardt, professor at the Athenaeum Illustre (now the University of
Amsterdam), to the Dutch Indies to promote scientific research. An artist draughtsman,
Jan Bik (1780–1872), joined Reinwardt on the expedition, and was commissioned to

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
versity of
Amsterdam), to the Dutch Indies to promote scientific research. An artist draughtsman,
Jan Bik (1780–1872), joined Reinwardt on the expedition, and was commissioned to
make natural history drawings. Two years later, the Dutch government sent the specially
selected Antoine Payen (1792–1853), a landscape painter from the Southern Netherlands
who would stay in the Indies until 1826.61 Payen executed most of his paintings after his
return to Europe. Until his death, Payen delivered paintings to the satisfaction of the
government, from Tournai where he lived, which meanwhile had become part of
Belgium. The paintings were meant to become part of a Gallery of the Indies in the
Hague, but was never built.62
                                     Humboldt’s Invention of the Natural Landscape     159

      Payen’s work has certain points in common with that of Hodges. Looking for the
grandiose and the spacious, he nevertheless remains within the classicist canon. Just like
Hodges, Payen had received his education from a classicist artist. Humboldt seems not
have noted Payen.
      In the second volume of Cosmos (1847), when Humboldt was 78 years old, he

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
ges, Payen had received his education from a classicist artist. Humboldt seems not
have noted Payen.
      In the second volume of Cosmos (1847), when Humboldt was 78 years old, he
mentions a couple of artists whom he thought had surpassed Hodges in vision. Among
them are Johann Moritz Rugendas and Ferdinand Bellermann, artists who had both
worked in Latin-America. Humboldt was in close contact with them, provided them
with letters of recommendation and also owned some of their work. The strength of their
work seems to lie primarily in their documentary qualities, similar to the qualities in the
work of some of the French artists under Humboldt’s patronage, Nicolas-Antoine
Tauney and Jean Baptiste Debret.
      A greater ambition was shown in the work of Humboldt’s most faithful pupil in the
realm of art: the American painter Frederic Edwin Church (1826–1900), who belonged
to the Hudson River School. Adherents to this school transferred the grandeur of the
history piece to the exotic landscape, and succeeded where 50 years before Hodges had
failed.63
      Church followed in Humboldt’s footsteps: much of his work originated in Mexico

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
story piece to the exotic landscape, and succeeded where 50 years before Hodges had
failed.63
      Church followed in Humboldt’s footsteps: much of his work originated in Mexico
and Ecuador. When Church first learned about Humboldt, he cancelled his trip to Italy,
an almost compulsory part of any artist’s training, and went to the tropics instead. Albert
Bierstadt (1830–1902), who also belonged to the Hudson River School, looked for the
exotic closer to home, in the United States itself, mostly in the Rocky Mountains.
      In genuine Humboldtian style, Church and Bierstadt combined sublime grandiosity
with painstaking detail on huge canvasses. On the foreground of Bierstadt’s Storm in the
Rocky Mountains, Mount Rosalie, flowers and plants are depicted with botanical accuracy,
in their natural habitats. Bathed in celestial light, the plants share in the heroic drama
of the landscape, dominated by ferocious mountain crags and clouds that bring Wagner
to mind. The painting brings meteorological, geological, and ecological phenomena
together in a grand overstatement.
      Yet, the scenery depicted could not be found anywhere in real nature; from a

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
ng brings meteorological, geological, and ecological phenomena
together in a grand overstatement.
      Yet, the scenery depicted could not be found anywhere in real nature; from a
topographical point of view they were incorrect. The landscape elements by themselves
were realistic enough, brought together in an ideal whole.64 Barbara Novak speaks of
Claudian compositorial conventions, immersed in an atmosphere à la Salvator Rosa.65
But the important difference from seventeenth-century works is that the ideal whole is
correct from an ecological point of view.
      One of Church’s large canvasses, Heart of the Andes (1859), was among the most
popular paintings of its time, together with Rocky Mountains, painted by Bierstadt in 1863.
They were similar to the then popular panoramas and served the same goal: to show the
public on the American east coast the peculiarities of the American scenery. The shows
were a huge success, and the visitors were filled with patriotic fervour. They saw an
unspoilt wilderness on the landscapes of Church and Bierstadt, a typically American
wilderness: everything bigger than in Europe. Most of the paintings feature no humans—

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
saw an
unspoilt wilderness on the landscapes of Church and Bierstadt, a typically American
wilderness: everything bigger than in Europe. Most of the paintings feature no humans—
the ones that are there look like tourists rather than settlers. Bierstadt painted all the
known places: Yosemite, Yellowstone, and the Grand Canyon. It was the beginning of a
trend. Bierstadt’s paintings and those by his follower Thomas Moran were instrumental in
obtaining the recognition of the sites as national parks. A big canvas by Moran, depicting
160          Chunglin Kwa

the Grand Canyon, was bought by Congress and was hung in a prominent place: the
Senate lobby on Capitol Hill.66
      Humboldt also exercised considerable influence on important European art
theoreticians, such as Karl Gustav Carus and John Ruskin. Ruskin’s efforts to show
that the aesthetic qualities of a certain area are brought forth by its physical (biological,
geological) features were directly inspired by Humboldt.67
      Carus, who as an artist was the pupil of Friedrich, was also the author of Neun
Briefe über die Landschaftsmalerei. In this work he develops, among other things, a

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf
text:
boldt.67
      Carus, who as an artist was the pupil of Friedrich, was also the author of Neun
Briefe über die Landschaftsmalerei. In this work he develops, among other things, a
‘‘physiognomics of mountains,’’ the terminology of which goes back to Humboldt. Carus
foresaw a new kind of art, an Erdlebenbildkunst. He meant by that a form of art that
faithfully renders natural phenomena and impregnates them at the same time with a
hidden, supernatural mystery.68 We could say that this is where Humboldt and Carus
parted ways, for Humboldt regarded as a scientific abstractum what Carus deemed
supernatural.
      Without belittling Humboldt’s influence on painting, we may say that he created
the romantic landscape primarily in and through his writings. Landscape paintings could
suggest, unlike any other medium, what could only be understood abstractly.
Henceforth, the word ‘‘landscape’’ would be this abstract whole, and a landscape
painting its interpretation on canvas.


                                     Acknowledgement
    The author thanks Adrienne Ciuffo, Jean-Marc Drouin, Feit en Fictie, Malcolm
Nicolson, Joes Segal and Jo Tollebeek for their comments.

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Alexander von Humboldt s invention of the natural landscape.pdf

    visual meaning the word had acquired at the time, namely, to designate the land as it could be
    apprehended from one point of view. But Humboldt used much more often words like site
    agreste, pays, lieux, for ensembles we would now call landscapes.

    Landscape (Oxford, 1979); John Dixon Hunt, ‘‘‘Ut pictura poesis’: The Garden and the

of Empire: Voyages, Botany and Representations of Nature, ed. David
    Philip Miller and Peter Hanns Reill (Cambridge, 1996), 258–92.
35. Humboldt, Ansichten der Natur, 11, 12.
36. Alexander von Humboldt, Personal Narrative of a Journey to the Equinoctial Regions of the
    New Continent, ed. Jason Wilson (Harmondsworth, 1995), 82; [translated from Relation

46. Botanist-geographers active in the first half of the nineteenth century, including Alphonse
    de Candolle, collected huge amounts of data that they presented in giant folded tables.
    The practitioners of this Tabellenstatistik had the feeling that they were still awaiting ‘‘their
    Newton.’’ See Janet Browne, The Secular Ark (New Haven, 1983).
